summary: The post examines the backlash when the video game Rust changed from giving all players white avatars to randomly assigning race. White players complained vocally about potentially being assigned black avatars, despite minorities having played as white characters without complaint for years. O'Neil notes the irony that white players would accept being a green alien but not a black man, and suggests games could further challenge players by including experiences like menstruation or pregnancy.
key_points: When Rust randomly assigned race to avatars, complaints and racist language increased dramatically from white players
Black and Hispanic gamers play more video games than whites but rarely complained about always being cast as white characters
White players would accept being a randomly colored alien but objected to being assigned a black avatar
The gaming industry is dominated by white male designers, which shapes default representation
O'Neil proposes games could be used educationally to help people experience different physical realities like menstruation
money_quotes: White people are okay with being cast as a green alien avatar, but no way they're going to be cast as a black man. WTF, white people?
They were not complaining about being cast as a white man in the initial version, because it's so common.
I'd love to see men in video games dealing with getting their period.
Those who advance to the next level can experience being pregnant and suffering sciatica. Or maybe even hot flashes and menopause, why not?
key_insight: The outrage over randomly assigned race in video games reveals that white identity is treated as the invisible default, while any other identity is seen as an imposition.
